subject: reading
title: Grade 3 Reading — EOG Practice Test 3
instructions: Read each selection carefully. Then answer the questions. Choose the one best answer for each question unless you are told to select more than one.

passages.title: The Kite Maker's Gift
passages.text:
Part 1

Every autumn, the wind swept down from the hills and rattled the windows of the little town. To most people it was just cold weather, but to Nina it was kite season. All year she saved bits of bright paper, thin sticks, and string in a wooden box beneath her bed.

This year Nina wanted to build the grandest kite the town had ever seen. She worked for three evenings, bending the sticks into a wide diamond and gluing red and gold paper across the frame. When she finally held it up, it was taller than she was.

"It will never fly," said her older brother Tomas, shaking his head. "It is far too big and heavy."

Nina frowned, but she did not give up. The next morning she carried her giant kite to the open field at the edge of town. She ran and ran, the string burning her hands, but the kite only bumped along the ground and tumbled into the dirt.

Part 2

Nina sat down in the grass, her shoulders low. A girl she did not know walked over and sat beside her. "My name is Priya," the girl said. "I just moved here. That is the most beautiful kite I have ever seen."

"It is beautiful," Nina sighed, "but it will not fly. It is too heavy for one person to lift into the wind."

Priya thought for a moment. "Maybe it does not need one person," she said. "Maybe it needs two."

The girls made a plan. Priya held the kite high above her head while Nina took the string and ran. When Nina shouted, Priya let go. This time the wind caught the wide paper wings, and the great kite climbed higher and higher into the gray autumn sky. People in the field stopped to point and cheer.

Nina laughed until her cheeks hurt. "I could never have done it alone," she told her new friend. From that day on, the two girls built every kite together, and each one flew higher than the last.

passages.title: The Clever Turtle and the Drought
passages.text:
Part 1

Long ago, when the rains stopped coming, the great forest grew dry and brown. The river shrank to a thin trickle, and at last only one small pond still held water. All the animals came to drink there, but they argued day and night over who should drink first.

"I am the strongest, so I should drink first!" roared the lion.

"I am the tallest, so I should drink first!" said the giraffe, stretching her long neck.

"I am the fastest!" cried the gazelle. The arguing grew so loud that no one drank at all, and the precious water sat untouched while the animals shouted.

A small turtle watched quietly from the muddy bank. She was not the strongest or the tallest or the fastest, and the others paid her no attention. But the turtle had something the others did not: a patient and thoughtful mind.

Part 2

"Friends," the turtle called out in her small voice. The animals stopped to listen, more out of surprise than respect. "If we keep fighting, the hot sun will dry the pond before anyone takes a single sip. Then we will all go thirsty."

The lion snorted, but he knew the turtle spoke the truth. "What do you suggest, little one?" he asked.

"Let us take turns," said the turtle. "The smallest animals can drink first, for they need only a little. The largest can drink last, for they take the most. That way the pond will not be stirred into mud before everyone has had a chance."

The animals saw the wisdom in her plan. One by one, from smallest to largest, they drank their fill. The water stayed clear and cool, and there was enough for everyone. From that day on, whenever the animals faced a hard problem, they no longer asked who was strongest. Instead, they asked, "What does the clever turtle think?"

passages.title: Mountains of Fire
passages.text:
Part 1

Deep beneath the ground you walk on, the Earth is far hotter than you might imagine. Many miles below the surface, rock becomes so hot that it melts into a thick, glowing liquid. This melted rock is called magma. Most of the time, the magma stays trapped far underground. But sometimes it finds a way to escape, and when it does, it can build a mountain of fire called a volcano.

The Earth's outer shell is not one solid piece. Instead, it is broken into giant pieces, like a cracked eggshell. These pieces are always moving, though far too slowly for us to feel. Where two pieces meet, magma can push its way up through the cracks between them. Over many years, the magma that reaches the surface piles up and hardens into the cone shape of a volcano.

Part 2

When the pressure deep inside a volcano grows too great, the volcano erupts. Magma bursts out of an opening at the top, and once it reaches the surface, it is given a new name: lava. Lava can flow slowly down the sides of the mountain like a river of fire, or it can shoot high into the air. Along with the lava come clouds of ash, hot gas, and chunks of rock.

Volcanoes can be dangerous, but they also do important work. The ash that settles on the ground breaks down over time into rich soil where plants grow well. Many islands in the ocean were built entirely by underwater volcanoes that erupted again and again until they rose above the waves. Scientists called volcanologists study volcanoes closely. By watching for small earthquakes and changes in the gases that leak out, they can often warn people before a volcano erupts, giving families time to move to safety.

passages.title: Wilma Rudolph: The Fastest Woman in the World
passages.text:
Part 1

Wilma Rudolph was born in 1940 in the state of Tennessee. She was a tiny baby, born too early, and she was sick again and again during her childhood. When she was just five years old, an illness called polio left her left leg weak and twisted. Doctors told her family that she might never walk normally again.

But Wilma and her mother refused to give up. Twice a week, her mother took her on a long bus ride to a hospital for treatments. At home, Wilma's brothers and sisters took turns rubbing her weak leg every single day. Slowly, with years of hard work, Wilma began to walk without the heavy metal brace she had once needed. By the time she was twelve, she could run and play like any other child.

Part 2

Once Wilma could run, she discovered she loved it more than anything. She joined her school's basketball and track teams and quickly became a star. She was so fast that her coach gave her a nickname: "Skeeter," because she buzzed around the track like a mosquito.

In 1960, Wilma traveled to Rome, Italy, to compete in the Olympic Games against the best runners in the world. There she did something amazing. She won three gold medals, becoming the first American woman ever to do so in a single Olympics. The crowds cheered for the young woman who had once been told she might never walk.

Wilma did not forget where she came from. After her racing days were over, she became a teacher and a coach. She started a program to help young athletes believe in themselves, just as she once had. Wilma Rudolph showed the world that with courage and hard work, a person can overcome almost any obstacle.

passages.title: The Lighthouse of Spring
passages.text:
Part 1

The old apple tree stands by the gate,
bare and gray through the winter's wait.
Its branches are empty, its bark is rough,
the cold winds blow, and the days are tough.

But deep in the wood, where no eye can see,
a secret is stirring inside the tree.
The roots drink rain from the softening ground,
and sap climbs up without a sound.

Part 2

Then one bright morning the buds appear,
little green promises, year after year.
The blossoms open, pink and white,
like tiny lanterns of soft spring light.

The bees come humming, the robins sing,
the whole tree glows with the gift of spring.
And I understand, as I stand and stare:
the tree was working all winter there.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, what does Nina keep in the wooden box beneath her bed?
options: Toys she no longer plays with
Books about flying kites
Bright paper, thin sticks, and string
Old photographs and letters
questions.explanation: Part 1 says all year Nina saved bits of bright paper, thin sticks, and string in a wooden box beneath her bed.

questions.questionText: What problem does Nina have at the end of Part 1?
options: Her brother takes her kite away.
Her giant kite is too heavy to lift into the wind.
The wind stops blowing across the field.
She runs out of paper before she can finish.
questions.explanation: At the end of Part 1, the kite only bumps along the ground because it is too big and heavy to rise.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "grandest" as it is used in Part 1?
options: Most colorful
Oldest
Most expensive
Most impressive and largest
questions.explanation: Nina wants the grandest kite the town had ever seen, and she builds one taller than she is, so grandest means the most impressive and largest.

questions.questionText: Which word best describes Nina based on the whole story?
options: Fearful
Selfish
Determined
Careless
questions.explanation: Nina keeps trying even after her brother doubts her and her first attempt fails, which shows she is determined.

questions.questionText: How do Nina and Priya finally get the kite to fly?
options: They tie the kite to the back of a bicycle.
They make the kite smaller and lighter.
They wait for a stronger wind to come.
Priya holds the kite up high while Nina runs with the string.
questions.explanation: In Part 2, Priya holds the kite high while Nina runs with the string, and when Priya lets go, the wind lifts it.

questions.questionText: What is the theme, or lesson, of this story?
options: Some problems can be solved by working together.
It is best to keep your ideas a secret from others.
You should never listen to your older brother.
Big kites are always better than small kites.
questions.explanation: Nina cannot fly the kite alone, but with Priya's help they succeed, showing that working together can solve a problem.

questions.questionText: How are Part 1 and Part 2 of the story connected?
options: Part 1 shows a problem Nina cannot solve alone, and Part 2 shows how a new friend helps her solve it.
Part 1 and Part 2 describe two completely different kites.
Part 1 explains a problem that is never solved in Part 2.
Part 2 happens many years before the events in Part 1.
questions.explanation: Part 1 sets up the problem that the kite is too heavy for one person; Part 2 shows Priya helping Nina solve it together.

questions.questionText: Which sentence from the text best shows that Nina did not give up after her kite failed the first time?
options: "Nina frowned, but she did not give up."
"A girl she did not know walked over and sat beside her."
"It will never fly," said her older brother Tomas.
"People in the field stopped to point and cheer."
questions.explanation: The sentence stating Nina frowned but did not give up directly shows she kept trying despite the failure.

questions.questionText: Why do all the animals come to the one small pond in Part 1?
options: It is the only place to find food.
It is the only place that still has water.
The lion has invited them all there.
They want to swim and play together.
questions.explanation: Part 1 says the rains stopped, the river shrank, and only one small pond still held water, so the animals came there to drink.

questions.questionText: Why does no one drink the water at first, even though they are thirsty?
options: The water is too dirty to drink.
They are waiting for the rain to return.
The turtle will not let them near the pond.
They are too busy arguing about who should go first.
questions.explanation: Part 1 says the arguing grew so loud that no one drank at all while the animals shouted about who should be first.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "precious" as it is used in Part 1?
options: Easy to find
Very valuable and important
Cold and clear
Dark and muddy
questions.explanation: The water is the only water left in a drought, so calling it precious means it is very valuable and important.

questions.questionText: What plan does the turtle suggest to solve the problem?
options: The animals should wait until night to drink.
Each animal should dig its own small pond.
The strongest animal should guard the water.
The animals should drink in turns, from smallest to largest.
questions.explanation: In Part 2, the turtle suggests the animals take turns drinking from smallest to largest so the water is not stirred into mud.

questions.questionText: Which word best describes the turtle in the story?
options: Greedy
Boastful
Frightened
Wise
questions.explanation: The turtle thinks carefully and comes up with a fair plan that helps everyone, which shows she is wise.

questions.questionText: What is the main lesson of this folktale?
options: Ponds should never be shared with other animals.
Strong animals will always get what they want.
It is better to keep quiet than to share an idea.
A good idea can matter more than being big or strong.
questions.explanation: The small turtle solves the problem the strong animals could not, showing that a good idea can matter more than size or strength.

questions.questionText: Which detail from the text best supports the idea that the animals came to respect the turtle?
options: "A small turtle watched quietly from the muddy bank."
"The lion snorted, but he knew the turtle spoke the truth."
"They asked, 'What does the clever turtle think?'"
"She was not the strongest or the tallest or the fastest."
questions.explanation: At the end, the animals begin asking what the clever turtle thinks whenever they face a problem, showing they came to respect her.

questions.questionText: Use both "The Kite Maker's Gift" and "The Clever Turtle and the Drought" to answer this question. What is one way Nina and the turtle are alike?
options: Both are the strongest character in their story.
Both solve a problem in a way the doubters did not expect.
Both win a race against faster animals.
Both build something out of paper and sticks.
questions.explanation: Nina succeeds though her brother doubted her, and the turtle solves the problem though the bigger animals ignored her; both succeed in ways others did not expect.

questions.questionText: According to Part 1, what is magma?
options: A type of hard rock found on islands
Melted rock found deep beneath the ground
A small earthquake near a volcano
A cloud of ash above a mountain
questions.explanation: Part 1 explains that far below the surface, rock melts into a thick, glowing liquid called magma.

questions.questionText: In Part 1, the author compares the Earth's outer shell to a "cracked eggshell." What does this comparison help the reader understand?
options: The outer shell is white and smooth.
The Earth is shaped exactly like an egg.
The Earth is very fragile and could break apart.
The outer shell is broken into giant moving pieces.
questions.explanation: The author says the shell is broken into giant pieces like a cracked eggshell, helping the reader picture separate moving pieces.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "erupts" as it is used in Part 2?
options: Bursts out with great force
Sinks back down underground
Slowly cools and hardens
Stops moving completely
questions.explanation: Part 2 says when pressure grows too great, the volcano erupts and magma bursts out, so erupts means bursts out with force.

questions.questionText: According to the text, what is melted rock called once it reaches the surface?
options: Magma
Ash
Soil
Lava
questions.explanation: Part 2 states that once magma reaches the surface, it is given a new name: lava.

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the ideas in Part 1 and Part 2 of the text?
options: Part 1 explains how a volcano forms underground, and Part 2 explains what happens when it erupts.
Part 1 and Part 2 describe two different mountains.
Part 2 gives the opposite opinion from Part 1.
Part 1 compares islands, and Part 2 compares oceans.
questions.explanation: Part 1 describes how magma builds a volcano; Part 2 explains the eruption, so the parts build on each other in order.

questions.questionText: What is the connection between volcanic ash settling on the ground and plants growing well?
options: It lists steps in time order only.
It presents a problem with no solution.
It shows cause and effect: the ash breaks down and makes rich soil where plants grow.
It compares two things that are alike.
questions.explanation: The ash settles and breaks down into rich soil (cause), which helps plants grow well (effect), a cause-and-effect connection.

questions.questionText: Select the two ways the text says volcanoes can be helpful.
options: They make the weather warmer all over the world.
They cause the giant pieces of Earth to stop moving.
They built many islands in the ocean.
Their ash breaks down into rich soil for plants.
questions.explanation: The text says volcanic ash forms rich soil and that many ocean islands were built by underwater volcanoes. The other choices are not stated.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of this text?
options: Lava always shoots high into the air during an eruption.
Islands are the safest place to watch a volcano.
Volcanoes form when hot magma escapes from inside the Earth, and they can be both dangerous and helpful.
Scientists cannot tell when a volcano will erupt.
questions.explanation: The text explains how volcanoes form from escaping magma and describes their dangers and benefits, which together form the main idea.

questions.questionText: Which detail from the text best supports the idea that scientists can help keep people safe from volcanoes?
options: "Most of the time, the magma stays trapped far underground."
"By watching for small earthquakes and changes in the gases, they can often warn people before a volcano erupts."
"Lava can flow slowly down the sides of the mountain like a river of fire."
"The Earth's outer shell is not one solid piece."
questions.explanation: The detail about scientists watching for warning signs and warning people before an eruption supports that they help keep people safe.

questions.questionText: In what state was Wilma Rudolph born?
options: Tennessee
Italy
Tennessee's capital city of Rome
California
questions.explanation: Part 1 says Wilma Rudolph was born in 1940 in the state of Tennessee.

questions.questionText: What happened to Wilma when she was five years old?
options: She joined a basketball team.
She won her first race.
An illness called polio left her left leg weak.
She moved to Italy with her family.
questions.explanation: Part 1 states that when she was five, polio left her left leg weak and twisted.

questions.questionText: How did Wilma's family help her get better?
options: Her mother took her for treatments and her siblings rubbed her leg every day.
They moved her to a warmer state.
They bought her a new metal brace each year.
They hired a famous track coach to train her.
questions.explanation: Part 1 says her mother took her for treatments twice a week and her brothers and sisters rubbed her weak leg every single day.

questions.questionText: Why did Wilma's coach give her the nickname "Skeeter"?
options: Because she was small as a baby
Because she buzzed around the track like a mosquito
Because she came from a place called Skeeter
Because she liked to play in the summer
questions.explanation: Part 2 explains the coach called her Skeeter because she buzzed around the track like a mosquito.

questions.questionText: What amazing thing did Wilma do at the 1960 Olympic Games?
options: She became the first woman to walk without a brace.
She became a teacher and a coach.
She won three gold medals in a single Olympics.
She started a program for young athletes.
questions.explanation: Part 2 says she won three gold medals, becoming the first American woman to do so in a single Olympics.

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the ideas in Part 1 and Part 2 of the text?
options: Part 2 explains a problem that Part 1 never mentions.
Part 1 and Part 2 describe two different runners.
Part 1 tells how Wilma overcame illness to walk, and Part 2 tells how she became a champion runner.
Part 1 gives the opposite opinion from Part 2.
questions.explanation: Part 1 shows Wilma learning to walk again; Part 2 builds on that by showing her become an Olympic champion.

questions.questionText: What is the connection between Wilma being told she might never walk and the crowds cheering for her in Rome?
options: It compares Wilma to the other Olympic runners.
It lists her medals in the order she won them.
It shows a problem and the steps used to fix a machine.
It shows a contrast: she overcame what doctors said she could never do.
questions.explanation: The text contrasts the doctors' warning that she might never walk with her later Olympic victory, showing how far she came.

questions.questionText: Select the two things Wilma Rudolph did AFTER her racing days were over.
options: She became a teacher and a coach.
She won three Olympic gold medals.
She learned to walk without a brace.
She started a program to help young athletes.
questions.explanation: Part 2 says that after her racing days, Wilma became a teacher and coach and started a program to help young athletes. Winning medals and learning to walk happened earlier.

questions.questionText: Which sentence from the text best supports the idea that Wilma wanted to help others after her racing days?
options: "She started a program to help young athletes believe in themselves."
"She won three gold medals, becoming the first American woman ever to do so."
"Wilma Rudolph was born in 1940 in the state of Tennessee."
"She was so fast that her coach gave her a nickname."
questions.explanation: The sentence about starting a program to help young athletes believe in themselves shows she wanted to help others.

questions.questionText: What is the poem mostly about?
options: How an apple tree changes from winter to spring
How to pick apples in the autumn
Why bees and robins like apple trees
A boy who plants a new apple tree
questions.explanation: The poem describes the bare apple tree in winter and then its buds and blossoms in spring, so it is mostly about the tree changing across seasons.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the phrase "little green promises" in Part 2?
options: Green ribbons tied to the branches
Buds that promise blossoms and fruit are coming
Little green insects on the leaves
Small green coins hidden in the tree
questions.explanation: The phrase describes the new buds, which are a sign, or promise, that blossoms and fruit will soon come.

questions.questionText: The blossoms are compared to "tiny lanterns of soft spring light." What does this comparison help the reader picture?
options: The bright, glowing way the blossoms look in the spring.
The blossoms are made of paper and string.
The blossoms are warm to the touch.
The blossoms light up at night like real lamps.
questions.explanation: Comparing blossoms to lanterns of light helps the reader picture how bright and glowing the blossoms look in spring.

questions.questionText: How are Part 1 and Part 2 of the poem different?
options: Part 1 is about a tree, and Part 2 is about a garden.
Part 1 rhymes, but Part 2 does not rhyme.
Part 1 describes the tree in winter, and Part 2 describes the tree in spring.
Part 1 describes daytime, and Part 2 describes nighttime.
questions.explanation: Part 1 shows the bare, gray tree in winter, while Part 2 shows the blossoming tree in spring.

questions.questionText: What does the speaker understand at the end of the poem?
options: Winter lasts longer than spring.
The tree was secretly working all through the winter.
Bees are the most important part of spring.
The tree should be cut down in spring.
questions.explanation: The last lines say the speaker understands the tree was working all winter there, even when it looked bare.

questions.questionText: Use both "The Clever Turtle and the Drought" and "The Lighthouse of Spring" to answer this question. What is one idea both texts share?
options: Both are poems with rhyming lines.
Both show that quiet, patient effort can lead to something good.
Both take place during a long, dry drought.
Both are mainly about animals racing one another.
questions.explanation: The turtle quietly and patiently solves a problem, and the tree quietly works all winter to bloom; both show patient effort leading to a good result.
